beginner.question: What is the syntax for a CSS media query?
beginner.answer: @media media-type and (condition) { CSS rules }. Example: @media screen and (max-width: 768px) { ... }

beginner.question: What are the media types available in CSS?
beginner.answer: Common media types are all, screen, print, speech. screen is the default for computer screens and mobile devices.

beginner.question: What does max-width: 768px mean in a media query?
beginner.answer: It applies the CSS rules when the viewport width is 768 pixels or less.

beginner.question: What does min-width: 768px mean in a media query?
beginner.answer: It applies the CSS rules when the viewport width is 768 pixels or more.

beginner.question: How do you combine multiple conditions in a media query?
What is orientation in media queries?
beginner.answer: orientation detects whether the device is in landscape (width > height) or portrait (height > width) mode.

beginner.question: How do you target landscape orientation with a media query?
beginner.answer: @media (orientation: landscape) { ... } applies styles when the device is in landscape mode.

beginner.question: What does prefers-reduced-motion check for?
beginner.answer: It checks if the user has requested reduced motion in their system accessibility settings to disable animations.

beginner.question: What does prefers-color-scheme detect?
beginner.answer: It detects whether the user prefers light or dark color scheme for theming purposes.

beginner.question: Can you nest media queries inside other CSS rules?
beginner.answer: Yes, CSS nesting allows media queries to be nested inside selectors for better organization.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between @media and @import for responsive CSS?
intermediate.answer: @media conditionally applies rules within a stylesheet; @import can load different stylesheets based on media, but is less performant.

intermediate.question: How do you use media queries for high-resolution displays?
intermediate.answer: Use min-resolution: 2dppx or -webkit-min-device-pixel-ratio: 2 for Retina/HiDPI screens.

intermediate.question: What is the prefers-contrast media feature?
intermediate.answer: It detects if the user prefers more or less contrast in their display settings, useful for accessibility adjustments.

intermediate.question: How do you write a media query for print styles?
intermediate.answer: @media print { ... } hides non-essential elements, adjusts colors, and ensures content flows well on paper.

intermediate.question: What is the prefers-reduced-data media feature?
intermediate.answer: It detects if the user prefers reduced data usage, allowing you to serve smaller images or fewer assets.

intermediate.question: How do you exclude a media type in a media query?
intermediate.answer: Use the not keyword: @media not screen { ... } applies to all non-screen media (e.g., print, speech).

intermediate.question: Can you use JavaScript to detect media queries?
intermediate.answer: Yes, using window.matchMedia('(min-width: 768px)') to programmatically check and respond to media query changes.

intermediate.question: How do media queries interact with CSS specificity?
intermediate.answer: Media queries do not affect specificity. Rules inside media queries have the same specificity as outside; the later rule wins if conditions match.

intermediate.question: What is the only keyword in media queries used for?
intermediate.answer: The only keyword prevents older browsers that do not support media queries from applying the styles: @media only screen and (min-width: 768px).

intermediate.question: How do you write media queries for hover capability?
intermediate.answer: @media (hover: hover) targets devices that support hovering (like mice), while (hover: none) targets touch devices.

advanced.question: How does the CSS media query cascade interact with framework-based responsive utilities?
advanced.answer: Framework classes often apply fixed breakpoint values. Custom media queries can override them if placed after and specificity matches or exceeds framework selectors.

advanced.question: What is the difference between prefers-reduced-motion and prefers-reduced-transparency?
advanced.answer: reduced-motion disables animations and transitions; reduced-transparency disables transparent/translucent effects for readability.

advanced.question: How do you handle media queries in CSS-in-JS solutions?
advanced.answer: CSS-in-JS libraries typically use template literals or helper functions to embed @media rules within component styles.

advanced.question: What is the interaction between media queries and CSS Container Queries?
advanced.answer: Container queries (@container) respond to container size, while media queries respond to viewport size. They can be combined for element-level and viewport-level responsive behavior.

advanced.question: How do you use range syntax in modern media queries?
advanced.answer: Modern syntax allows @media (width >= 768px) and (width <= 1200px) instead of min-width/max-width for more readable range expressions.

advanced.question: What are the performance considerations for too many media queries?
advanced.answer: Hundreds of media queries can increase stylesheet size and parse time. Grouping related breakpoints together improves maintainability and performance.

advanced.question: How does scripting media feature (prefers-scripting) work?
advanced.answer: It detects if JavaScript is enabled/disabled, allowing you to provide fallback styles for non-JS environments.

advanced.question: What is the forced-colors media feature?
advanced.answer: It detects if the user has forced colors mode enabled (like Windows High Contrast mode) for accessibility.

advanced.question: Can you use custom properties in media query conditions?
advanced.answer: No, custom properties (CSS variables) cannot be used in media query conditions because @media conditions are evaluated before custom property resolution.

advanced.question: How do you test media queries for dynamic viewport units like svw, lvw, dvw?
advanced.answer: Media queries still use the initial containing block (viewport) dimensions. Dynamic viewport units (dvh, dvw) respond to browser UI changes but media queries use the static viewport.
